posts.text:
ICE wants to put a massive immigrant warehouse in a town in Georgia but the mostly Trump-supporting community is having none of it. The facility would suck resources away from residents. So the city put a lock on the water until they get some answers. 🎯

cbsnews.com/atlanta/news/soc…

This is a beta version. A larger dataset we're building includes cause of death, the specific hospitals where people died, and more. We plan to publish it on detentionreports.com. If you see errors or have additional information, let us know. 

austinkocher.substack.com/p/…

It is remarkable that Congress has not acted. The administration has ignored the pattern entirely. Rather than investigating why people are dying at this rate in government custody, they've treated each death as an isolated incident. The data tells a different story.

ICE's own press releases frame these deaths in language that shifts blame onto the deceased, leading with criminal history and immigration status while avoiding any institutional accountability. We've found cases where the official press releases appear inaccurate/incomplete.

The map shows 22 locations across the country. South Florida alone accounts for 8 deaths across four facilities. El Paso has seen 4. Adelanto in California, 3. These deaths are geographically dispersed, not concentrated in one or two problem sites.

felt.com/map/ICE-Detention-D…

We archived all 44 ICE press releases announcing deaths in custody, extracted structured data from each one, and mapped the facilities where people were last detained. We excluded 3 deaths: one person who fled custody and two killed by a sniper at the Dallas field office.

41 people have died in ICE detention since January 2025. Twelve since January 1 alone, roughly one every six days. Adam Sawyer and I built a beta of an interactive map of detained deaths using ICE's own press releases. 🧵

The Hagerstown ruling is a temporary pause not a permanent stop. The legal fight continues. I've covered this story from the ground up. Read my full reporting and analysis here:
 austinkocher.substack.com/p/…
 austinkocher.substack.com/p/…

This is one facility in a much larger plan. ICE's "Detention Reengineering Initiative" aims to convert 24 large warehouses nationwide into detention centers by Sept. 30, 2026. The program has $45 billion authorized through 2029.

The TRO lasts 14 days, after which the judge will decide whether to extend it. In the meantime, the federal government has the option to prepare an Environmental Impact Statement. ICE had planned the facility to be operational by September 2026.

On March 11, U.S. District Judge Brendan Hurson agreed. He issued a Temporary Restraining Order, immediately halting all construction and renovations. The court found DHS did not appear to have taken a "hard look" at environmental consequences.

The warehouse sits next to Semple Run, a stream that feeds into the Conococheague Creek and ultimately the Potomac River. The AG argued construction would cause sediment runoff threatening state-protected and endangered species.

Maryland AG Anthony Brown filed a 28-page lawsuit on Feb. 23 arguing DHS violated the National Environmental Policy Act purchasing and renovating the site without an environmental review or any public comment process.

The federal government has now spent at least $215 million on the site: $102.4M to purchase it, and a $113M renovation contract awarded to KVG LLC of Gettysburg, PA with options that could grow to $642 million over 3 years.

If operated at that capacity, the detained population would effectively double the size of Williamsport. The city of Hagerstown has already said it doesn't have the water capacity to support a facility of this size.

When I visited the site in late February, the scale was striking. It's an enormous metal and concrete box with bay doors built for loading tractor trailers. The building currently has 4 toilets and 2 water fountains. ICE plans to house 1,500 people there.

The facility isn't actually in Hagerstown. It's in Williamsport, MD, a town of 2,000 people. ICE purchased the 54-acre, 825,000-sq-ft warehouse on Jan. 16, 2026 for $102.4 million. It was built as a logistics hub not to house human beings.

A federal judge just blocked construction of the proposed ICE detention facility near Hagerstown, MD. I've been on the ground at this site. Here's what you need to know about what this building is, how we got here, and what happens next.
A government that withholds data on who it's detaining, where, and under what conditions cannot be held accountable. I am trying to do the work to change that: austinkocher.substack.com

Michigan's Attorney General filed a FOIA request in March 2026 demanding basic information about ICE's warehouse purchase in Romulus, including renovation plans, intended use, and impact assessments ICE had not disclosed.
